entries.name: Antigua
entries.desc: The larger of a two-island nation in the Leeward Islands, ringed by numerous sheltered bays and beaches, with a deep sailing tradition centered on the historic English Harbour.

photo.alt: Nelson's Dockyard and marina at English Harbour, Antigua
photo.credit: <!-- photo: nelsons-dockyard-english-harbour-antigua.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Antigua English Harbour Nelson's Dockyard 1.jpg" by Dr. Thomas Liptak · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Antigua_English_Harbour_Nelson%27s_Dockyard_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Aruba
entries.desc: A dry, wind-swept island in the southern Caribbean within the Dutch Kingdom, known for consistent sunshine, white-sand western beaches, cactus-studded desert terrain, and reliable trade winds.

photo.alt: Wind-shaped fofoti (divi-divi) tree on Eagle Beach, Aruba
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fofoti-tree-eagle-beach-aruba.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Fofoti Tree, Eagle Beach, Aruba (28568601953).jpg" by Jason Boldero · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fofoti_Tree,_Eagle_Beach,_Aruba_(28568601953).jpg -->

entries.name: The Bahamas
entries.desc: An Atlantic archipelago of 700 islands north of Cuba, known for the shallow turquoise Exumas, colonial Nassau on New Providence, the pink-sand beaches of Harbour Island, and blue-hole diving in Andros.

photo.alt: Pink-sand beach on Harbour Island, Bahamas
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pink-sands-beach-harbour-island-bahamas.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Pink Sands Beach, Harbour Island, Bahamas (5279465868).jpg" by Mike's Birds · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pink_Sands_Beach,_Harbour_Island,_Bahamas_(5279465868).jpg -->

entries.name: Barbados
entries.desc: The easternmost Caribbean island, an independent nation with a distinct British-influenced culture, calm west-coast beaches, rugged Atlantic surf on the east, and a strong rum-distilling heritage.

photo.alt: Paynes Bay beach on Barbados' west coast
photo.credit: <!-- photo: paynes-bay-barbados.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Paynes Bay Barbados.jpg" by Barry Haynes · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Paynes_Bay_Barbados.jpg -->

entries.name: Vancouver Island
entries.desc: A large island off Canada's Pacific coast in British Columbia, home to the provincial capital Victoria, temperate old-growth rainforest, surf beaches at Tofino, and whale-watching waters.

photo.alt: Long Beach near Tofino, Vancouver Island
photo.credit: <!-- photo: long-beach-tofino-vancouver-island.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Long beach - Tofino - Vancouver Island - 10.jpg" by Buiobuione · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Long_beach_-_Tofino_-_Vancouver_Island_-_10.jpg -->

entries.name: Cayman Islands
entries.desc: A British Overseas Territory of three islands south of Cuba, anchored by Grand Cayman with Seven Mile Beach, clear diving walls, and the famous Stingray City sandbar where southern stingrays gather in shallow water.

photo.alt: Seven Mile Beach, Grand Cayman
photo.credit: <!-- photo: seven-mile-beach-grand-cayman.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Seven Mile Beach, Grand Cayman - panoramio - James Willamor.jpg" by James Willamor · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seven_Mile_Beach,_Grand_Cayman_-_panoramio_-_James_Willamor.jpg -->

entries.name: Easter Island (Rapa Nui)
entries.desc: A remote Chilean island in the southeastern Pacific, one of the world's most isolated inhabited places, famous for the hundreds of monumental moai statues carved by its Polynesian Rapa Nui people.

photo.alt: Moai statues at the Rano Raraku quarry, Easter Island
photo.credit: <!-- photo: moai-rano-raraku-easter-island.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Moai at Rano Raraku (Easter Island).jpg" by Mc vc · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Moai_at_Rano_Raraku_(Easter_Island).jpg -->

entries.name: Cook Islands
entries.desc: A small self-governing South Pacific nation in free association with New Zealand, centered on the lush main island of Rarotonga and the lagoon of Aitutaki, with a low-key Polynesian pace.

photo.alt: Ngatangiia Bay on Rarotonga, Cook Islands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ngatangiia-bay-rarotonga-cook-islands.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "VIEW OF NGATANGIIA BAY ON RAROTONGA.jpg" by Jerrye and Roy Klotz MD · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:VIEW_OF_NGATANGIIA_BAY_ON_RAROTONGA.jpg -->

entries.name: Curaçao
entries.desc: A southern Caribbean island off Venezuela, part of the Dutch Kingdom, noted for the pastel colonial waterfront of Willemstad, arid landscapes, and sheltered reef-fringed coves.

photo.alt: Colorful colonial buildings along the Handelskade waterfront in Willemstad
photo.credit: <!-- photo: willemstad-waterfront-curacao.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "The colorful buildings of the Handelskade in Willemstad, Curaçao" by Matthew T Rader · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_colorful_buildings_of_the_Handelskade_in_Willemstad,_Cura%C3%A7ao.jpg -->

entries.name: Faroe Islands
entries.desc: A self-governing North Atlantic archipelago within the Kingdom of Denmark, known for steep sea cliffs, grass-roofed villages, frequent fog and rain, and large seabird colonies.

photo.alt: Steep sea cliffs at Vestmannabjørgini in the Faroe Islands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: vestmanna-cliffs-faroe-islands.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Vestmannabjørgini, Faroe Islands" by Erik Christensen · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vestmannabj%C3%B8rgini,_Faroe_Islands.JPG -->

entries.name: Fiji
entries.desc: A South Pacific nation of hundreds of islands, most people living on the two largest, known for coral reefs and diving, palm-fringed beaches, and small outer-island resorts.

photo.alt: Palm-fringed beach in Fiji
photo.credit: <!-- photo: beach-fiji.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Fiji-8" by Jason Baker · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fiji-8_(16524236002).jpg -->

entries.name: Corsica
entries.desc: A mountainous French island in the western Mediterranean with its own strong regional identity, prized for the demanding GR20 hiking trail, forested peaks, and clear-water coastal beaches.

photo.alt: Calanques de Piana coastal cliffs in Corsica
photo.credit: <!-- photo: calanques-de-piana-corsica.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Calanques de Piana, Corsica" by Michal Osmenda · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Calanques_de_Piana,_Corsica_(8132779554).jpg -->

entries.name: Bora Bora
entries.desc: A small volcanic island in French Polynesia's Leeward group, ringed by a turquoise lagoon and coral barrier reef, centered on the peaks of Mount Otemanu and known for overwater bungalow resorts.

photo.alt: Mount Otemanu rising above the Bora Bora lagoon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mount-otemanu-bora-bora.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Mount Otemanu, Bora Bora - French Polynesia" by Sergio Calleja · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mount_Otemanu,_Bora_Bora_-_French_Polynesia.jpg -->

entries.name: Moorea
entries.desc: A heart-shaped volcanic island near Tahiti in French Polynesia, marked by jagged green peaks, two deep bays, a surrounding lagoon, and a quieter atmosphere than nearby Bora Bora.

photo.alt: Opunohu Bay and Cook's Bay seen from the Belvedere lookout in Moorea
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cooks-bay-moorea.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Opunohu Bay and Cook's Bay Seen From Belvedere Lookout in Moorea" by Michael R Perry · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Opunohu_Bay_and_Cook%27s_Bay_Seen_From_Belvedere_Lookout_in_Moorea_(6597138321).jpg -->

entries.name: Crete
entries.desc: The largest Greek island, birthplace of the Bronze Age Minoan civilization, with the palace of Knossos, mountainous gorges, long beaches, and a strong regional food and wine tradition.

photo.alt: Ruins of the Palace of Knossos in Crete
photo.credit: <!-- photo: knossos-palace-crete.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Knossos palace" by Theofanis Ampatzidis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Knossos_palace.jpg -->

entries.name: Mykonos
entries.desc: A Cycladic island in the Aegean known for its whitewashed cubic architecture, iconic windmills above the port, the canal-side Little Venice waterfront, internationally known beach clubs, and access to the ancient sanctuary island of Delos.

photo.alt: Whitewashed windmills on a hillside above Mykonos town, overlooking the sea
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mykonos-windmills.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Windmills in Mykonos 02.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Windmills_in_Mykonos_02.jpg -->

entries.name: Santorini
entries.desc: A crescent-shaped Cycladic island formed by a flooded volcanic caldera, known for whitewashed cliff-top villages, blue-domed churches, dark volcanic beaches, and celebrated sunset views over the Aegean.

photo.alt: Blue-domed churches and whitewashed buildings cascading down the cliffside in Oia, Santorini
photo.credit: <!-- photo: oia-santorini.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "View of Oia, Santorini 01.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:View_of_Oia,_Santorini_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Iceland
entries.desc: A North Atlantic island nation on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, shaped by active volcanoes, glaciers, geysers, and waterfalls, with the northern lights visible in the darker months.

photo.alt: Skógafoss waterfall dropping over a mossy cliff face in southern Iceland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: skogafoss-waterfall-iceland.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Skogafoss waterfall - Iceland - Landscape photography (34692750741).jpg" by Giuseppe Milo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Skogafoss_waterfall_-_Iceland_-_Landscape_photography_(34692750741).jpg -->

entries.name: Bali
entries.desc: An Indonesian island where Hinduism shaped the culture, known for terraced rice fields, volcanic peaks, numerous temples, surf breaks, and a long-established international tourism scene.

photo.alt: Green terraced rice fields in Jatiluwih with volcanic peaks rising behind, Bali
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bali-rice-terraces.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Jatiluwih Rice Terraces - 2015.02 - panoramio.jpg" by rheins · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Jatiluwih_Rice_Terraces_-_2015.02_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Komodo Island
entries.desc: A rugged Indonesian island within Komodo National Park, best known as habitat of the Komodo dragon, with dry savanna hills, pink-sand beaches, and rich surrounding dive waters.

photo.alt: A Komodo dragon on the dry ground of Komodo National Park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: komodo-dragon.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Komodo Dragon in Komodo Island 07.jpg" by Hotel Kaesong · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Komodo_Dragon_in_Komodo_Island_07.jpg -->

entries.name: Capri
entries.desc: A small rocky island in the Bay of Naples long favored as a resort, known for dramatic sea cliffs, the sea cave called the Blue Grotto, and the towering Faraglioni rock stacks.

photo.alt: The Faraglioni rock stacks rising from the sea off Capri's coast, with a sailboat passing by
photo.credit: <!-- photo: capri-faraglioni.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Capri Faraglioni with boat.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Capri_Faraglioni_with_boat.jpg -->

entries.name: Sardinia
entries.desc: The second-largest Mediterranean island, an Italian autonomous region with its own Sardinian language, known for the turquoise Costa Smeralda resort coast, prehistoric nuraghe stone towers scattered across the interior, and mountainous highland villages.

photo.alt: Turquoise water and a sandy beach along Sardinia's Costa Smeralda, with yachts anchored offshore
photo.credit: <!-- photo: costa-smeralda-sardinia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Sardegna, costa smeralda - panoramio (2).jpg" by Silvia Franceschetti · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sardegna,_costa_smeralda_-_panoramio_(2).jpg -->

entries.name: Sicily
entries.desc: The largest Mediterranean island, dominated by the active volcano Mount Etna, layered with Greek, Roman, Arab, and Norman heritage, and known for baroque towns and a distinctive cuisine.

photo.alt: Mount Etna's smoking summit rising above the Sicilian countryside
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mount-etna-sicily.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Etna calm again - panoramio.jpg" by Deborah Cordwell · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Etna_calm_again_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Jamaica
entries.desc: A Greater Antilles island nation and birthplace of reggae music, known for the Blue Mountains interior, Dunn's River Falls, the resort strip of Montego Bay, and jerk cooking rooted in Maroon culture.

photo.alt: The Blue Mountains rising over the Jamaican interior
photo.credit: <!-- photo: blue-mountains-jamaica.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Blue Mountains, Jamaica" by Wolmadrian · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Blue_Mountains,_Jamaica.jpg -->

entries.name: Langkawi
entries.desc: The main island of a Malaysian archipelago in the Andaman Sea, a duty-free destination noted for rainforest-clad hills, a cable car and sky bridge, mangroves, and quiet beaches.

photo.alt: Langkawi Sky Bridge suspended above the rainforest canopy
photo.credit: <!-- photo: langkawi-sky-bridge.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Langkawi sky bridge" by Flickr user "The Dilly Lama" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Langkawi_sky_bridge.jpg -->

entries.name: Maldives
entries.desc: A low-lying Indian Ocean nation of coral atolls and hundreds of small islands, known for overwater villas, clear lagoons, and reefs, with many resorts occupying their own private island.

photo.alt: A resort island ringed by coral reef and turquoise lagoon in the Maldives
photo.credit: <!-- photo: maldives-resort-island.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Maldives-resort-island" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Maldives-resort-island.jpg -->

entries.name: Malta
entries.desc: A small island nation in the central Mediterranean south of Sicily, with one of Europe's densest concentrations of heritage — Baroque Valletta (UNESCO), the prehistoric Ħaġar Qim temples, and the medieval hilltop city of Mdina — plus clear diving waters.

photo.alt: Panoramic view of Baroque Valletta across the Grand Harbour
photo.credit: <!-- photo: valletta-panorama-malta.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Panorama of Valletta" by Olga Prystai · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Panorama_of_Valletta.jpg -->

entries.name: Mauritius
entries.desc: A volcanic Indian Ocean island nation east of Madagascar with a diverse multicultural population, lagoon-fringed beaches protected by a coral reef, sugarcane plains, and forested inland peaks.

photo.alt: Blue Bay's coral-fringed lagoon on the Mauritius coast
photo.credit: <!-- photo: blue-bay-mauritius.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Blue Bay Mauritius" by Lukas von Daeniken · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Blue_Bay_Mauritius_(23456132492).jpg -->

entries.name: New Zealand (South Island)
entries.desc: The larger and less populated of New Zealand's two main islands, defined by the Southern Alps, glaciers, fjords such as Milford Sound, alpine lakes, and the adventure hub of Queenstown.

photo.alt: Milford Sound fjord framed by the Southern Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: milford-sound-south-island.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Milford Sound, New Zealand (13)" by Michal Klajban · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Milford_Sound,_New_Zealand_(13).JPG -->

entries.name: Palawan
entries.desc: A long, narrow Philippine province known for the karst islands and lagoons of El Nido and Coron, the Puerto Princesa underground river, and extensive coral reefs and dive sites.

photo.alt: Karst limestone islands rising from El Nido Bay, Palawan
photo.credit: <!-- photo: el-nido-bay-palawan.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "El Nido Bay, islands, Palawan, Philippines" by Vyacheslav Argenberg · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:El_Nido_Bay,_islands,_Palawan,_Philippines.jpg -->

entries.name: Madeira
entries.desc: A subtropical Portuguese island in the Atlantic west of Morocco, known for its mild year-round climate, mountainous terrain, levada irrigation-channel walking trails, and fortified Madeira wine.

photo.alt: Levada walking trail through forest in Madeira
photo.credit: <!-- photo: levada-da-serra-do-faial-madeira.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Levada da Serra do Faial - Madeira 03" by H. Zell · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Levada_da_Serra_do_Faial_-_Madeira_03.jpg -->

entries.name: Puerto Rico
entries.desc: A United States territory in the Greater Antilles, home to historic Old San Juan, the El Yunque rainforest, bioluminescent bays, and a lively Spanish-Caribbean culture.

photo.alt: Colorful colonial-era street in Old San Juan
photo.credit: <!-- photo: old-san-juan-colorful-street-puerto-rico.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Colourful colonial-era houses and cobblestone streets - Old San Juan (5421844245)" by Jorge Láscar · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Colourful_colonial-era_houses_and_cobblestone_streets_-_Old_San_Juan_(5421844245).jpg -->

entries.name: St. Lucia
entries.desc: A volcanic Windward Islands nation known for the twin Pitons rising from the sea near Soufrière, rainforest interiors, hot springs, and secluded resort coves.

photo.alt: The twin Pitons rising from the sea near Soufrière
photo.credit: <!-- photo: the-pitons-soufriere-saint-lucia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "The Pitons at Soufriere Saint Lucia" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Pitons_at_Soufriere_Saint_Lucia.jpg -->

entries.name: Seychelles
entries.desc: An Indian Ocean archipelago northeast of Madagascar, known for granite boulder beaches, endemic wildlife including the giant coco de mer palm, and nature reserves protecting rare species.

photo.alt: Granite boulders on a beach at Anse Source d'Argent, La Digue
photo.credit: <!-- photo: anse-source-d-argent-la-digue-seychelles.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Anse Source d'Argent 2-La Digue" by Tobias Alt · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Anse_Source_d%27Argent_2-La_Digue.jpg -->

entries.name: Canary Islands
entries.desc: A Spanish volcanic archipelago in the Atlantic off northwest Africa, enjoying a mild year-round climate and crowned by Mount Teide, Spain's highest peak, on the island of Tenerife.

photo.alt: Mount Teide seen across the volcanic landscape of Tenerife
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mount-teide-tenerife-canary-islands.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Vista del Teide desde el mirador de Chio, Santa Cruz de Tenerife, España, 2012-12-16, DD 01" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vista_del_Teide_desde_el_mirador_de_Chio,_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife,_Espa%C3%B1a,_2012-12-16,_DD_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Ibiza
entries.desc: A Balearic island off Spain's eastern coast, known for its UNESCO-listed walled old town of Dalt Vila, internationally famous open-air nightclubs, sheltered northern coves, and a quieter agritourism side away from the resort areas.

photo.alt: Aerial view of the walled old town of Dalt Vila above Ibiza harbor
photo.credit: <!-- photo: dalt-vila-ibiza.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Dalt Vila 004" by Xaviduran · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dalt_Vila_004.JPG -->

entries.name: Mallorca
entries.desc: The largest of Spain's Balearic Islands, combining the historic port capital of Palma, the rugged Serra de Tramuntana mountain range, hillside villages, and a long stretch of coastal beaches and coves.

photo.alt: Coastal view of the Serra de Tramuntana mountain range
photo.credit: <!-- photo: serra-de-tramuntana-mallorca.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Serra de Tramuntana - 5" by Maria Rosa Ferre · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Serra_de_Tramuntana_-_5.jpg -->

entries.name: Sri Lanka
entries.desc: A large island nation south of India combining ancient Buddhist ruins, hill-country tea plantations, wildlife safaris, and southern beaches, with a monsoon pattern that shifts the best coast by season.

photo.alt: Sigiriya rock fortress rising above the surrounding jungle
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sigiriya-rock-fortress-sri-lanka.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sigiriya Sri Lanka" by Roshita Trashani · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sigiriya_Sri_Lanka.jpg -->

entries.name: Zanzibar
entries.desc: A semi-autonomous archipelago off the Tanzanian coast, historically a spice and trading hub, centered on the old quarter of Stone Town and fringed by white-sand beaches and reefs.

photo.alt: Stone Town waterfront skyline seen from the water, Zanzibar
photo.credit: <!-- photo: stone-town-waterfront-zanzibar.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Stone Town-2.jpg" by Fanny Schertzer · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stone_Town-2.jpg -->

entries.name: Koh Samui
entries.desc: Thailand's second-largest island in the Gulf of Thailand, known for its palm-fringed beaches (Chaweng, Lamai), the Big Buddha temple, a full-moon party beach at nearby Koh Phangan, and the quieter national park island of Koh Tao for diving.

photo.alt: Big Buddha Temple (Wat Phra Yai) complex, Koh Samui
photo.credit: <!-- photo: big-buddha-temple-koh-samui.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Big Buddha Temple (Wat Phra Yai).jpg" by Maksim Sundukov · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Big_Buddha_Temple_(Wat_Phra_Yai).jpg -->

entries.name: Phuket
entries.desc: Thailand's largest island, connected to the mainland by bridge, with west-coast beach resorts, a historic old town of Sino-Portuguese shophouses, and boat access to the limestone islands of Phang Nga Bay.

photo.alt: Ko Tapu limestone karst (James Bond Island), Phang Nga Bay
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ko-tapu-phang-nga-bay.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Ko Tapu (James Bond) Island in Phang Nga Bay, Thailand.jpg" by Vyacheslav Argenberg · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ko_Tapu_(James_Bond)_Island_in_Phang_Nga_Bay,_Thailand.jpg -->

entries.name: Big Island (Hawaiʻi)
entries.desc: The youngest and largest Hawaiian island, where active lava flows from Kīlauea in Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park, snow caps Mauna Kea (4,207 m), and the landscape shifts from black lava desert on the west coast to lush rainforest in the east.

photo.alt: Lava fountain erupting from Kīlauea, Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kilauea-lava-fountain-hawaii-volcanoes-np.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Episode 14 (2025-03-20) of Kilauea's December 2024 eruption; 200-meter fountain.jpg" by USGS/M. Patrick · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Episode_14_(2025-03-20)_of_Kilauea%27s_December_2024_eruption;_200-meter_fountain.jpg -->

entries.name: Kauai
entries.desc: The oldest and most geologically eroded Hawaiian island, dominated by the dramatic sea cliffs of the Nā Pali Coast, the red-rock Waimea Canyon, the wettest spot on Earth at Mount Waiʻaleʻale, and calm north-shore beaches at Hanalei.

photo.alt: Fluted sea cliffs of the Nā Pali Coast, Kauai
photo.credit: <!-- photo: na-pali-coast-kauai.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Na Pali Coast Kauai Hawaii (32406276598).jpg" by dronepicr · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Na_Pali_Coast_Kauai_Hawaii_(32406276598).jpg -->

entries.name: Maui
entries.desc: The second-largest Hawaiian island, known for the dormant Haleakalā volcano and its crater, the winding Road to Hana, resort beaches, and seasonal humpback whale watching.

photo.alt: Haleakalā volcano summit crater with cinder cones, Maui
photo.credit: <!-- photo: haleakala-crater-maui.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Summit of Mount Haleakala Crater Maui Hawaii (31869172538).jpg" by dronepicr · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Summit_of_Mount_Haleakala_Crater_Maui_Hawaii_(31869172538).jpg -->

entries.name: Oahu
entries.desc: The most populated Hawaiian island, home to Honolulu, Waikiki Beach, the Pearl Harbor National Memorial, and Diamond Head crater, with the legendary big-wave surf breaks of the North Shore active in winter months.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Diamond Head crater with Waikiki and Honolulu, Oahu
photo.credit: <!-- photo: diamond-head-oahu.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Diamond Head Hawaii - panoramio.jpg" by Eric Tessmer · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Diamond_Head_Hawaii_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: U.S. Virgin Islands
entries.desc: A United States territory of three main islands — St. Thomas for sailing and duty-free shopping, St. Croix with Danish colonial architecture and rum distilleries, and St. John where two-thirds of the land is protected national park.

photo.alt: Trunk Bay beach and turquoise water, St. John, U.S. Virgin Islands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: trunk-bay-st-john-us-virgin-islands.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "StJohnTrunkBay.jpg" by Ben Whitney · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:StJohnTrunkBay.jpg -->
